titleStmt.title: Testimonies to John of Ephesus's Ecclesiastical History, Part 3 from the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian

editionStmt.p: This is a new translation of all passages in the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian which either 1) are introduced as quotations of the third part of the Ecclesiastical History (henceforth EH) of John of Ephesus, or 2) bear a close resemblance to passages found in the EH, or 3) can serve to close gaps in the sole manuscript of the EH. For the production of the edition on which this translation is based, Silvio Roggo identified the passages through a comparison of the Chronicle to the EH and transcribed them from the manuscript.

This new digital translation has been created within the context of the DFG Project "Commentary on John of Ephesus's Ecclesiastical History." The current version contains passages from the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian related to Book 1 of the EH. It should be considered preliminary, as it will be updated throughout the duration of the project. Philip Michael Forness translated the text from the Silvio Roggo's new edition of the relevant passages from Michael the Great's Chronicle produced within the context of the project. The translation benefitted enormously from the help of Silvio Roggo, Hartmut Leppin, and Nikolai Kiel who offered feedback, suggestions, and corrections as the translation was produced. Persons, places, and bible passages have been tagged by Philip Michael Forness, Silvio Roggo, and the project's student assistants (Katharina Koch, Marie Skubowius, and Eliano Veronesi).

availability.licence: Available under a Creative Commons Attribution ShareAlike 4.0 International License

cRefPattern.p: For references to the Peshitta Old and New Testaments, PES is used as the abbreviation.
Noted are references to a biblical book, chapter, verse, sc. PES:Gn:1:1; also possible: PES:Gn:1:1-3 for a sequence of verses or LXX:Gn:1:1.3 for two verses not following each other.

The following abbreviations are used: Gn, Ex, Lv, Num, Dt, Jos, Judg, Rt, 1Sa, 2Sa, 1Ko, 2Ko, 1Chr, 2Chr, 3Esr, Esr, Est, Jdt, Tob, 1Mak, 2Mak, 3Mak, 4Mak, Ps, Oden, Prov, Eccl, Song, Job, Wis, Sir, PsSal, Hos, Am, Mi, Joel, Ob, Jon, Nah, Hab, Zeph, Hag, Sach, Mal, Is, Jr, Bar, Lam, EpistJer, Hes, Sus, Dn, Bel; Mt, Mk, Lk, Jn, Act, Rom, 1Cor, 2Cor, Gal, Eph, Phil, Col, 1Th, 2Th, 1Tim, 2Tim, Tt, Phm, Heb, Jak, 1P, 2P, 1Jn, 2Jn, 3Jn, Jud, Rev.
Noted are references to a biblical book and chapter, sc. PES:Gn:1; also possible: PES:Gn:1-3 for a sequence of chapters or PES:Gn:1.3 for two chapters not following each other.
Noted are references to a biblical book, sc. PES:Gn.

interpretation.p: This file is encoded according to the guidelines of the Patristic Text Archive (PTA). See https://pta.bbaw.de/project/encoding-guidelines and https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3737666.
Biblical quotations are annotated and referenced. References follow the scheme declared above.

Persons are annotated and referenced with IDs according to the TIPNR - Tyndale Individualised Proper Names with all References list for biblical persons and IDs of PTA persons index or of Syriaca.org for all other persons.
Groups of persons and organisations are not annotated.
Places are annotated and referenced with Pleiades Gazetteer or Syriaca.org IDs.
Words are not tagged and not analyzed.

taxonomy.bibl: Text Type Classification by "Computational Historical Semantics
Patristic Text Archive Status of edition

category.catDesc: Critical edition in which the text-critical variants have been encoded.

Critical edition in which the text-critical variants have not been encoded, e.g. because it is a retro-digitisation of a printed critical edition and for copyright reasons the apparatus cannot be included in the retro-digitisation.
Edition which notes text-critical variants, but does not, for example, comprehensively include the manuscripts.
Pre-modern editions (e.g. editions in the Patrologia Graeca or Latina)

taxonomy.bibl: Patristic Text Archive Status of annotation

category.catDesc: All instances of a phenomenon (e.g. biblical quotations, persons, places) are encoded with the respective element.
Some instances of a phenomenon (e.g. biblical quotations, persons, places) are encoded with the respective element.
No instances of a phenomenon (e.g. biblical quotations, persons, places) are encoded with the respective element.
All encoded instances of a phenomenon (e.g. biblical quotations, persons, places) are identified with the respective norm data.
Some encoded instances of a phenomenon (e.g. biblical quotations, persons, places) are identified with the respective norm data
No encoded instances of a phenomenon (e.g. biblical quotations, persons, places) are identified with the respective norm data.

taxonomy.bibl: Patristic Text Archive Status of linguistic analysis

div.p: At the beginning of the third book of the Ecclesiastical History of John of Asia, he speaks in this way: “At the beginning of the narrative of this book, the groans of Jeremiah’s Lamentations are needed, which he voiced in regard to Jerusalem so that we might also cry out in the same way regarding those things which have happened in the church of God and regarding the terrors that suddenly sprung on all the faithful in the sixth year of Justin III.”

For a time of forty years the orthodox were in serenity, including in the imperial city, and their communities gathered in freedom of assembly. Then during the days of the fast, John of Sirmin through his cruelty awakened the emperor in a rage against them. He ordered their gathering places to be seized, the altars to be torn out, the priests and their bishops to be apprehended and cast into prison.

From that moment on, as so to speak a vehement storm and tempest with dense dark clouds battering down hail, so did a storm swell up and arise against the calm ship of the holy church of the orthodox, and they were cast into different places of confinement. Some were in prison with murderers, others in praetoria , others in a dekanikon , still others in prison cells under the episcopal palace, others in other places of banishment within the churches. Many fled and were scattered about in all manner of places. What is more grievous is that they ordained the devout bishops once again and demanded that they produce a libellus . Words do not suffice to speak about those things that Christians endured in places of confinement. What is more grievous is that they did not allow anyone the imprisoned bishops knew to come to them or to speak with them at all. Food was given
The Syriac word for “food” is taken as masculine in this passage
to them, preserving life. They made those who were confined in monasteries - if it is right for them to be called monasteries - endure more tribulations, looking at them with ire, casting the rinsing liquid from their basins at them, giving them instead of wine some vinegar to drink, spoiled wine, or the putrid sediment from foul dregs, so that they could not live.

As for the old man Stephen, a presbyter and man who was adorned with divine zeal, when they had confined him, he outright pronounced a curse against them to their face. In their fury, they handed him over to the prefect and he said to the prefect: “Evil and wicked one, why do you keep as a Christian alongside those who sent me to you, judging the Christians as a pagan? You are not among the living, unless you immediately set me on fire, a Christian old man, and eat me when I have been roasted.” When the prefect heard these things, he was shaken and sent him to the bishop. That one in a rage sent him into imprisonment in Heraclea in Thrace. He was imprisoned for two years without anyone at all of those who knew him being allowed to be seen by him. As he was not given a garment to change into, he was tormented by lice and grew ill to the point of death. He pronounced a curse so that the synodites would not bury him and so that none of them would offer a prayer for him. God prepared some Romans who were orthodox, and they buried him.

John sent for and had brought to him a certain bishop, a simple old man, whose name was Paul, and he cast him in fetters. While subjecting him by force, he issued an order that he be reappointed as bishop of Antioch in Caria. As though they thought they had defrocked him of the original priestly rank, they reappointed him as if he were a lay person. Such disgrace even came from his own people, and they called him “the twice-dipped.” He died from his grief.

While John of Sirmin was constraining Elisha, Elisha said: “As for me, since I am worthy, I have been ordained bishop. But as for you, you are not going to ordain me in any way. But if you still deem it orderly to depose me from the priesthood or to reordain me, then depose me also of baptism.” John of Sirmin said to him deceitfully: “I am only placing the cloak on you.” Since he was not persuaded, he sent him to a place of banishment.

John then brought Stephen out of prison after
Stephen had endured many torments from him. Stephen
strongly rebuked him regarding the second ordination. For this reason,
John then cast him in prison and sent the quaestionarii. They beat him with harsh blows until blood gushed forth. After these things,
John then brought him and tormented him so that he might accept the second ordination. But he shouted and said: “O bia!
The Syriac word is a transliteration of the Greek exclamation ὦ βία.
I will never accept to be stripped of the priesthood, unless you first also strip me of baptism.” Then there was a great commotion, and Stephen was able to go to the emperor. He cried out: “Christianity has been lost! It has been lost! The ordinances and canons have been corrupted! Look, I have been a bishop for twenty years. But now that I have given myself over to acquiesce to your command, becoming blameworthy, he wants to depose me from the orthodox priesthood and reordain me as one of his own. Let him reveal from which canons he has it! Either he was not aware of and did not know the ecclesiastical canons, or although he knew them, he is disregarding them in his tyranny. Look, he is casting the blame on your majesty. If he at your order is doing these things, then order him to abolish the baptism as well and to baptize again. The nineteenth canon of the 318 fathers, concerning the heresy of Paul of Samosata and those like him, orders them to be rebaptized and then to become priests. Let him reveal from where he came to depose us so that he might reappoint us.” The emperor was startled as though from sleep. He despised this deed, ordered that it not come to pass, and immediately made a typos pragmatikos so that no one might venture to abolish the priesthood, except for heresies concerning which the canons give an order.

Then, when John of Sirmin saw that his servant, the persecutor of the orthodox, had perished, he again went to the emperor and began inflaming him even more against the orthodox. But the emperor, in a rage, said to him: “Are the ones whom you are intent on having me persecute pagans?” He said to him: “No.” Then he said to him: “Are they heretics?” He said: “This is not the case, my lord.” The emperor said: “So, you have just testified yourself that they are Christians. How then are you going to persuade me to become a persecutor of Christians as Diocletian and the others? Go sit in your church and be silent! Do not burden us.”
